name: StarDist
description: Deep-learning-based 2D/3D nucleus and object detection using star-convex polygons. Ideal for segmenting round or roughly convex objects such as cell nuclei in fluorescence microscopy images.
input_data: Single-channel or multi-channel 2D/3D fluorescence images with round or convex objects (e.g. DAPI-stained nuclei)
output_data: Label image where each detected object has a unique integer ID, ROI set, and object count
use_when: You need to segment round or roughly convex objects like nuclei, cells, or organelles in fluorescence images, especially when objects are densely packed or touching
do_not_use_when: Objects are highly elongated, irregular, or non-convex (e.g. neurons, filaments). Not suitable for brightfield or H&E images without retraining
typical_use_cases: Counting DAPI-stained nuclei in tissue sections
Segmenting GFP-labeled cells in culture
Detecting spots or puncta in fluorescence images
Quantifying nuclear morphology in high-throughput screens

name: MorphoLibJ
description: Collection of mathematical morphology tools and plugins for ImageJ. Includes morphological filtering, watershed segmentation, geodesic distance transform, label image operations, and region analysis.
input_data: Grayscale or binary 2D/3D images, or label images from prior segmentation
output_data: Filtered images, label images, distance maps, Results tables with region measurements (area, perimeter, shape descriptors, intensity statistics)
use_when: You need morphological operations (opening, closing, erosion, dilation), watershed segmentation, connected component analysis, label image manipulation, or region-based measurements
do_not_use_when: You need deep-learning-based segmentation or simple global thresholding — use StarDist or Auto Threshold instead
typical_use_cases: Separating touching objects with marker-controlled watershed
Measuring shape descriptors (circularity, elongation) of segmented regions
Morphological filtering to remove noise or fill holes
Geodesic reconstruction for extracting connected foreground regions
Analyzing label images from other segmentation tools

name: CLIJ2
description: GPU-accelerated image processing library using OpenCL. Provides fast implementations of common operations like filtering, thresholding, labeling, and measurements on the GPU.
input_data: Any 2D/3D image that can be processed by ImageJ (grayscale, binary, label images)
output_data: Processed images (same types as input), Results tables with measurements
use_when: Processing is too slow on CPU, you have large images or batch processing needs, or you want to speed up filtering/thresholding/labeling pipelines
do_not_use_when: No compatible GPU is available, images are small and processing is already fast, or you need operations not implemented in CLIJ2
typical_use_cases: Accelerating Gaussian blur on large 3D stacks
Fast batch thresholding and labeling of many images
GPU-accelerated connected component analysis
Building fast processing pipelines in macro/script

name: BioVoxxel Toolbox
description: Collection of tools for biological image analysis including extended particle analyzer, watershed variants, neighborhood analysis, binary feature extractor, and speckle inspector.
input_data: Binary or grayscale 2D images, typically from prior thresholding or segmentation
output_data: Filtered binary images, Results tables with particle measurements, neighborhood analysis statistics
use_when: You need advanced particle filtering (by shape, size, circularity), neighborhood/proximity analysis between objects, binary feature extraction, or speckle inspection
do_not_use_when: You need basic particle analysis (use built-in Analyze Particles), deep-learning segmentation, or 3D analysis
typical_use_cases: Filtering segmented objects by multiple shape criteria simultaneously
Analyzing spatial relationships between different cell populations
Extracting binary features like convex hull deficiency
Inspecting and removing speckle noise from binary masks

name: BigStitcher
description: Tool for alignment and fusion of large multi-tile, multi-angle, multi-channel microscopy datasets. Handles terabyte-scale datasets with interest point-based registration.
input_data: Multi-tile, multi-angle, or multi-channel 3D microscopy datasets (e.g. light-sheet, confocal tile scans), typically in HDF5/N5 format
output_data: Stitched/fused single image or virtual fusion for visualization, transformation parameters
use_when: You have tiled acquisitions, multi-view light-sheet data, or large datasets that need stitching with sub-pixel precision
do_not_use_when: You have simple 2D tile grids (use Grid/Collection Stitching), single-view single-tile data, or small datasets that fit in memory
typical_use_cases: Stitching confocal tile scans of whole tissue sections
Fusing multi-view light-sheet microscopy data
Registering and merging terabyte-scale cleared tissue datasets
Aligning multi-channel acquisitions with different orientations

name: BoneJ
description: Plugin suite for bone image analysis. Includes tools for measuring bone thickness, connectivity, anisotropy, and fractal dimension from micro-CT images.
input_data: 3D binary or grayscale micro-CT images of bone or similar porous structures
output_data: Results tables with measurements (thickness, connectivity density, anisotropy degree, fractal dimension), thickness maps, skeleton images
use_when: You need to quantify bone microarchitecture from micro-CT scans, measure trabecular thickness, analyze porosity, or assess structural connectivity
do_not_use_when: Images are not bone/porous structures, data is 2D only, or you need soft tissue analysis
typical_use_cases: Measuring trabecular thickness and spacing in micro-CT bone samples
Quantifying bone volume fraction (BV/TV)
Analyzing connectivity and anisotropy of trabecular networks
Assessing scaffold porosity in tissue engineering

name: Bio-Formats
description: Library for reading and writing life sciences image file formats. Supports over 150 formats including proprietary microscopy formats (Zeiss, Leica, Nikon, Olympus, etc.).
input_data: Microscopy image files in proprietary or open formats (.czi, .lif, .nd2, .oib, .ome.tiff, .vsi, etc.)
output_data: ImagePlus objects with full metadata (pixel size, channel info, timestamps, instrument settings)
use_when: You need to open proprietary microscopy formats, preserve metadata during import, export to OME-TIFF, or access multi-series files
do_not_use_when: Files are standard formats already supported by ImageJ (TIFF, PNG, JPEG) and you don't need metadata preservation
typical_use_cases: Opening Zeiss .czi, Leica .lif, or Nikon .nd2 files with full metadata
Importing multi-position, multi-timepoint datasets as separate series
Converting proprietary formats to OME-TIFF for archiving
Extracting acquisition metadata (pixel size, exposure, objective) from image files

name: Labkit
description: Intuitive interactive pixel classification and segmentation tool. Uses random forest classifier with fast GPU-accelerated preview for labeling 2D and 3D images.
input_data: 2D or 3D grayscale or multi-channel images where manual annotation of a few example regions is feasible
output_data: Segmentation label image, trained classifier model (reusable on similar images)
use_when: You need interactive pixel classification with real-time preview, want to train a classifier by painting labels, or need segmentation that adapts to your specific image type
do_not_use_when: Objects have well-defined intensity thresholds (use Auto Threshold), you need instance segmentation of individual objects (use StarDist), or fully automated batch processing without user interaction
typical_use_cases: Segmenting tissue regions in histology images by painting examples
Classifying pixels into foreground/background in complex images
Training a reusable classifier for batch segmentation of similar images
Interactive segmentation of 3D volumes slice-by-slice

name: LimeSeg
description: 3D segmentation plugin based on surface evolution with superellipsoids. Designed for segmenting cells in 3D fluorescence microscopy images, especially epithelial tissues.
input_data: 3D fluorescence images with membrane-labeled or surface-labeled cells, especially confocal z-stacks of epithelial tissues
output_data: 3D surface meshes of segmented cells, volume and surface area measurements
use_when: You need 3D cell segmentation in epithelial tissues, have membrane-stained confocal data, or want surface mesh outputs for 3D shape analysis
do_not_use_when: Cells are not membrane-labeled, images are 2D, objects are not roughly ellipsoidal, or you need nucleus segmentation (use StarDist 3D)
typical_use_cases: Segmenting cells in 3D confocal images of epithelial monolayers
Measuring cell volume and surface area in developing embryos
Analyzing cell packing geometry in tissues
3D shape analysis of membrane-labeled cells

name: SNT
description: Semi-automated neuron tracing and analysis framework. Traces neurons in 2D/3D, performs Sholl analysis, and supports SWC format for neuronal morphology reconstruction.
input_data: 2D or 3D fluorescence images of neurons (confocal, two-photon, light-sheet), or pre-existing SWC reconstruction files
output_data: SWC morphology files, Sholl analysis plots and tables, branch statistics, dendritic tree visualizations
use_when: You need to trace neuronal arbors, reconstruct dendritic trees, perform Sholl analysis, or quantify branching morphology
do_not_use_when: You need to trace non-neuronal filaments (use FilamentDetector), want fully automatic neuron segmentation without manual guidance, or are analyzing non-branching structures
typical_use_cases: Tracing and reconstructing dendritic arbors from confocal z-stacks
Performing Sholl analysis to quantify neuronal complexity
Comparing neuronal morphology between experimental conditions
Exporting neuron reconstructions in SWC format for simulation

name: FeatureJ
description: Feature detection and extraction tools based on image derivatives. Includes edge detection (Canny), Hessian-based feature detection, Laplacian, and structure tensor computation.
input_data: 2D grayscale images (8-bit or 32-bit)
output_data: Edge maps, Hessian eigenvalue images, Laplacian images, structure tensor images (orientation, coherence)
use_when: You need edge detection (Canny), ridge/tubular structure enhancement (Hessian), blob detection (Laplacian), or local orientation analysis (structure tensor)
do_not_use_when: You need simple thresholding-based edge detection, 3D feature detection, or deep-learning-based feature extraction
typical_use_cases: Detecting edges in microscopy images using Canny edge detector
Enhancing tubular structures (vessels, filaments) with Hessian eigenvalues
Detecting blob-like structures with Laplacian of Gaussian
Computing local orientation of fibrous structures with structure tensor

name: CSBDeep / N2V
description: Deep-learning-based image restoration including CARE (Content-Aware Image Restoration) and Noise2Void self-supervised denoising. Restores noisy, low-SNR microscopy images.
input_data: Noisy 2D/3D fluorescence microscopy images (low-SNR, underexposed, fast acquisitions)
output_data: Denoised/restored images with improved signal-to-noise ratio, preserving structural details
use_when: Images are noisy due to low light, fast acquisition, or low laser power, and you want to improve image quality without losing structural detail
do_not_use_when: Images already have good SNR, noise is caused by artifacts rather than photon statistics, or you need real-time processing (model inference can be slow without GPU)
typical_use_cases: Denoising low-light live-cell imaging data
Restoring images acquired with minimal laser exposure to reduce phototoxicity
Self-supervised denoising with Noise2Void when no paired training data is available
Improving image quality for downstream segmentation or analysis

name: TurboReg / StackReg
description: Image registration plugins for aligning image stacks. TurboReg performs subpixel registration of pairs of images; StackReg aligns time series using rigid body, affine, or bilinear transforms.
input_data: 2D image stacks or time-lapse sequences where frames need alignment (e.g. drifting timelapse)
output_data: Aligned/registered image stack with corrected drift or motion
use_when: You need to correct drift in time-lapse sequences, align sequential 2D images, or stabilize video microscopy data
do_not_use_when: You need elastic/non-rigid registration (use bUnwarpJ), 3D volume registration, or multi-tile stitching (use BigStitcher)
typical_use_cases: Correcting XY drift in live-cell time-lapse imaging
Aligning serial sections for reconstruction
Stabilizing hand-held or vibrating microscopy videos
Registering before/after treatment image pairs

name: bUnwarpJ
description: Elastic/consistent image registration plugin using B-spline deformations. Performs simultaneous registration of source and target images with regularization constraints.
input_data: Pairs of 2D images that need non-rigid alignment (e.g. tissue sections with deformations)
output_data: Registered image, deformation field, inverse deformation field
use_when: You need non-rigid/elastic registration to account for tissue deformation, stretching, or warping between images
do_not_use_when: Simple rigid or affine alignment is sufficient (use StackReg), images are 3D, or you need to register multiple tiles (use BigStitcher)
typical_use_cases: Registering histology serial sections with tissue deformation
Aligning images from different imaging modalities (correlative microscopy)
Correcting non-linear distortions between acquisition sessions
Mapping deformation fields for mechanical analysis

name: ClearVolume
description: Real-time GPU-based volume rendering plugin for Fiji. Provides interactive 3D visualization of volumetric datasets with transfer function editing and multi-channel support.
input_data: 3D image stacks (single or multi-channel confocal, light-sheet, or micro-CT volumes)
output_data: Interactive 3D volume rendering (visual display, screenshots, video recordings)
use_when: You want real-time interactive 3D visualization of volumetric data with GPU-accelerated rendering and transfer function control
do_not_use_when: You need 2D visualization, surface mesh rendering (use SciView), quantitative measurements from 3D data, or don't have a compatible GPU
typical_use_cases: Visualizing confocal z-stacks in 3D with adjustable transparency
Exploring multi-channel 3D fluorescence datasets interactively
Creating 3D volume renderings for presentations and publications
Quick 3D preview of light-sheet microscopy data

name: SciView
description: 3D visualization tool for scientific data in Fiji using the scenery rendering library. Supports meshes, volumes, point clouds, and VR headsets for immersive visualization.
input_data: 3D volumes, surface meshes (OBJ, STL), point clouds, or combinations thereof
output_data: Interactive 3D scene with rendered objects (visual display, screenshots, VR experience)
use_when: You need to visualize surface meshes, point clouds, or combined mesh+volume scenes, or want VR-based immersive visualization
do_not_use_when: You only need simple volume rendering (use ClearVolume for faster setup), 2D visualization, or quantitative analysis rather than visualization
typical_use_cases: Visualizing segmented cell surfaces as 3D meshes
Combining volume rendering with surface mesh overlays
Exploring 3D data in virtual reality with a VR headset
Rendering point cloud data from localization microscopy

name: OMERO Insight
description: Integration between Fiji and OMERO image management server. Browse, import, and export images between Fiji and OMERO for centralized image data management.
input_data: Images stored on an OMERO server, or local images to upload to OMERO
output_data: Images opened in Fiji from OMERO, or images uploaded to OMERO with associated metadata and annotations
use_when: Your lab uses an OMERO server for centralized image management and you need to open, analyze, or upload images between Fiji and OMERO
do_not_use_when: You don't have access to an OMERO server, images are stored locally, or you need to read proprietary formats directly (use Bio-Formats)
typical_use_cases: Browsing and opening images from an OMERO server in Fiji
Uploading processed images and ROIs back to OMERO
Running analysis on OMERO-hosted datasets from Fiji
Attaching analysis results to OMERO image annotations

name: N5 Viewer
description: Plugin for reading N5, Zarr, and HDF5 datasets in Fiji via BigDataViewer. Supports multi-scale pyramid datasets for efficient visualization of large images.
input_data: N5, Zarr, or HDF5 datasets on local disk or cloud storage (AWS S3), including multi-scale pyramids
output_data: BigDataViewer visualization of the dataset, or ImagePlus for further processing
use_when: You need to open large N5, Zarr, or HDF5 datasets that are too big for memory, or want to browse multi-scale pyramid datasets efficiently
do_not_use_when: Files are small enough to open directly, data is in standard image formats (TIFF, PNG), or you don't need lazy/out-of-core loading
typical_use_cases: Browsing terabyte-scale electron microscopy datasets stored in N5
Viewing Zarr datasets from cloud storage
Opening multi-resolution pyramid images for efficient navigation
Loading HDF5 datasets from computational pipelines

name: Rolling Ball Background Subtraction
description: Built-in ImageJ tool for subtracting uneven illumination background. Uses a rolling ball algorithm with configurable radius to correct for non-uniform background intensity.
input_data: 2D grayscale images with uneven background illumination or intensity gradients
output_data: Background-corrected image with more uniform intensity across the field of view
use_when: Images have uneven illumination, vignetting, or intensity gradients that would interfere with thresholding or intensity measurements
do_not_use_when: Background is already uniform, you need more sophisticated illumination correction (use flat-field correction), or the signal structures are larger than the rolling ball radius
typical_use_cases: Correcting uneven illumination before thresholding fluorescence images
Removing background gradients in brightfield microscopy
Preprocessing images for consistent intensity quantification
Removing vignetting artifacts from widefield microscopy

name: Auto Local Threshold
description: Collection of local thresholding methods including Bernsen, Contrast, Mean, Median, MidGrey, Niblack, Otsu, Phansalkar, and Sauvola. Essential for segmenting images with uneven illumination.
input_data: 8-bit grayscale 2D images with uneven illumination or spatially varying background
output_data: Binary image (black and white mask) where foreground is separated from background
use_when: Global thresholding fails due to uneven illumination, spatially varying background, or local contrast differences across the image
do_not_use_when: Illumination is uniform across the image (use Auto Threshold for global methods), you need instance segmentation (use StarDist), or images are already binary
typical_use_cases: Segmenting text or features with uneven illumination using Phansalkar or Sauvola
Thresholding fluorescence images with spatially varying background
Binarizing brightfield images with uneven lighting
Segmenting structures in images where no single global threshold works

name: Auto Threshold
description: Collection of global thresholding methods including Otsu, Huang, Li, Yen, Triangle, MaxEntropy, RenyiEntropy, and more. Provides 16+ automatic threshold algorithms.
input_data: 8-bit or 16-bit grayscale 2D images with relatively uniform illumination
output_data: Binary image (black and white mask), or the computed threshold value
use_when: You need automatic binarization of grayscale images with relatively uniform background, or want to compare multiple thresholding algorithms to find the best one
do_not_use_when: Illumination is uneven across the image (use Auto Local Threshold), you need instance segmentation (use StarDist), or manual threshold selection is preferred
typical_use_cases: Automatic binarization of fluorescence images with Otsu's method
Comparing multiple threshold methods (Otsu, Huang, Li, Triangle) to find the optimal one
Batch thresholding with a consistent automatic method
Segmenting bright objects on dark background with Triangle method

name: OrientationJ
description: Plugin for analyzing local orientation and coherence in images. Computes orientation maps, coherence maps, and distribution of orientations for fibrous or textured structures.
input_data: 2D grayscale images containing fibrous, filamentous, or textured structures with directional features
output_data: Orientation map (color-coded by angle), coherence map, orientation histogram/distribution, dominant direction angle
use_when: You need to analyze the orientation, alignment, or anisotropy of fibrous or textured structures in images
do_not_use_when: Structures have no directional features, you need 3D orientation analysis, or you want to segment individual fibers (use FilamentDetector)
typical_use_cases: Measuring collagen fiber alignment in tissue sections
Quantifying actin stress fiber orientation in cell cultures
Analyzing the degree of alignment in electrospun nanofiber scaffolds
Determining dominant orientation of muscle fibers or plant cell walls

name: Shape Filter
description: Filters connected components in binary images based on shape descriptors like area, circularity, elongation, solidity, and more. Useful for post-segmentation object selection.
input_data: Binary 2D images with segmented objects (connected components)
output_data: Filtered binary image containing only objects matching the shape criteria
use_when: You need to filter segmented objects by shape (circularity, elongation, solidity, area, convexity) to keep only objects of interest
do_not_use_when: You need to perform the initial segmentation (use thresholding or StarDist first), or you only need simple area-based filtering (use built-in Analyze Particles)
typical_use_cases: Removing elongated debris while keeping round cells after segmentation
Selecting only cells with specific circularity values
Filtering out small noise particles and large aggregates by area and shape
Isolating objects with specific morphological properties for further analysis

name: Measure Stack
description: Measures intensity statistics across all slices in an image stack. Generates per-slice measurements including mean, min, max, standard deviation, and integrated density.
input_data: Image stacks (z-stacks or time-lapse sequences), optionally with ROI selections
output_data: Results table with per-slice measurements (mean, min, max, StdDev, integrated density, area)
use_when: You need intensity measurements across all slices of a stack or time-lapse, rather than measuring only the current slice
do_not_use_when: You only need to measure a single 2D image (use Measure), or you need per-object measurements (use Analyze Particles)
typical_use_cases: Measuring fluorescence intensity changes over time in time-lapse data
Quantifying signal intensity across z-stack slices
Tracking intensity dynamics in ROIs across a movie
Generating intensity profiles along the z-axis

name: CLIJ Assistant
description: Interactive GUI for building GPU-accelerated image processing workflows. Records operations as scripts (Groovy, Python, etc.) for reproducible GPU processing pipelines.
input_data: Any 2D/3D image that can be opened in ImageJ
output_data: Processed image plus auto-generated script (Groovy, Python, JavaScript, or macro) recording all operations
use_when: You want to interactively build a GPU-accelerated processing pipeline and automatically generate a reproducible script from it
do_not_use_when: You already know the pipeline and can write it directly in a script, no GPU is available, or you need operations not in CLIJ2
typical_use_cases: Prototyping image processing workflows with instant GPU-accelerated preview
Generating reproducible scripts from interactive image processing sessions
Building GPU-accelerated batch processing pipelines for non-programmers
Exploring CLIJ2 operations with visual feedback before scripting

name: Colocalization Threshold
description: Performs pixel-based colocalization with automatic threshold determination using Costes' method. Generates scatterplots, colocalized pixel maps, and statistical coefficients.
input_data: Two-channel (or dual-color) fluorescence images where you want to assess signal overlap between channels
output_data: Colocalization coefficients (Pearson's, Manders', overlap), scatterplot, colocalized pixel map, Costes significance test p-value
use_when: You need to quantify the degree of spatial overlap between two fluorescent labels, determine if colocalization is statistically significant, or compute standard colocalization metrics
do_not_use_when: You need object-based colocalization (proximity of discrete objects), have more than two channels to compare simultaneously, or images have significant bleedthrough that hasn't been corrected
typical_use_cases: Measuring colocalization of two proteins labeled with different fluorophores
Computing Pearson's and Manders' coefficients for two-channel images
Determining statistical significance of colocalization with Costes' method
Generating scatterplots and colocalized pixel maps for publications

name: CellCounter
description: Manual cell counting tool with multiple counters. Allows marking and counting objects in images with different categories, exporting counts and positions.
input_data: Any 2D image or stack where objects need to be counted manually
output_data: Marker positions (XY coordinates), counts per category, exportable marker files
use_when: Automatic counting methods fail or are unreliable, you need to manually count and categorize objects, or you need ground truth annotations for validating automated methods
do_not_use_when: Objects can be reliably counted automatically (use StarDist or Analyze Particles), you have many images to process (use automated counting), or objects are too dense to mark individually
typical_use_cases: Manually counting cells in images where automated detection is unreliable
Counting and categorizing different cell types in tissue sections
Creating ground truth annotations for training or validating automated counters
Counting objects in complex images where thresholding fails

name: Watershed Irregular Features
description: Advanced watershed segmentation for separating touching irregular-shaped objects. Better than standard watershed for non-round objects like elongated cells or tissue regions.
input_data: Binary 2D images with touching or overlapping irregular-shaped objects
output_data: Binary image with separated objects, each object isolated by watershed lines
use_when: Standard watershed over-segments elongated or irregular objects, and you need to separate touching non-round objects
do_not_use_when: Objects are round (use Distance Transform Watershed), objects are well-separated, or you need 3D watershed segmentation
typical_use_cases: Separating touching elongated cells that standard watershed splits incorrectly
Splitting clustered irregular-shaped tissue regions
Separating touching objects in binary masks where shape is non-circular
Post-processing binary segmentation to split merged objects

name: Manual Tracking
description: Manual point-and-click tracking of objects over time. Generates tracks with XY coordinates per frame and computes velocity and distance measurements.
input_data: Time-lapse 2D image stacks with moving objects to track
output_data: Track data (XY coordinates per frame), velocity, distance, and trajectory plots
use_when: You need to track a small number of objects manually with high accuracy, automated tracking fails, or you need ground truth tracks
do_not_use_when: You have many objects to track (use automated tracking like TrackMate), objects are too numerous for manual annotation, or tracks span hundreds of frames
typical_use_cases: Tracking individual cell migration in wound healing assays
Manually tracking organelle movement in live-cell movies
Creating ground truth tracks for validating automated tracking
Measuring velocity and directionality of a few cells over time

name: IJ-OpenCV
description: Bridge between ImageJ and OpenCV for advanced computer vision operations. Enables using OpenCV algorithms (feature matching, contour detection, etc.) from within Fiji.
input_data: Any ImageJ image (ImagePlus) that you want to process with OpenCV algorithms
output_data: Processed images, detected features, contours, or matched keypoints, converted back to ImageJ format
use_when: You need computer vision algorithms from OpenCV (SIFT, SURF, template matching, contour analysis, Hough transforms) that are not natively available in ImageJ
do_not_use_when: The operation you need is already available natively in ImageJ/Fiji, or you can use a dedicated ImageJ plugin for the task
typical_use_cases: Using OpenCV feature matching (SIFT/ORB) for image registration
Applying Hough transform for line or circle detection
Advanced contour detection and analysis using OpenCV methods
Leveraging OpenCV's machine learning classifiers from within Fiji

name: Results to Excel
description: Exports ImageJ Results tables directly to Excel (.xlsx) format. Preserves all measurement data, formatting, and supports batch export of multiple tables.
input_data: ImageJ Results tables from any measurement or analysis operation
output_data: Excel (.xlsx) file containing the results data with formatting preserved
use_when: You need to export measurement results to Excel format for downstream analysis, reporting, or sharing with colleagues who use Excel
do_not_use_when: CSV output is sufficient (use File > Save As), you need programmatic data processing (use CSV/TSV), or no Results table exists
typical_use_cases: Exporting particle analysis measurements to Excel for statistical analysis
Saving intensity measurements from multiple images into a single Excel workbook
Creating formatted Excel reports of image analysis results
Batch exporting results tables from automated analysis pipelines

name: Sholl Analysis
description: Quantitative analysis of neuronal arbor morphology using the Sholl method. Counts intersections of neuronal processes with concentric circles/spheres at increasing radii from the soma.
input_data: 2D/3D images of traced neurons (binary skeletons or traced paths), or SWC reconstruction files
output_data: Sholl profile (intersections vs. distance), critical radius, ramification index, polynomial fit, Sholl decay
use_when: You need to quantify neuronal complexity, branching patterns, or compare dendritic arbor morphology between conditions using the Sholl method
do_not_use_when: You need to trace neurons first (use SNT for tracing), structures are not branching/tree-like, or you need non-Sholl morphometric analysis
typical_use_cases: Quantifying dendritic complexity in Golgi-stained neurons
Comparing neuronal branching between wild-type and mutant conditions
Analyzing Sholl profiles from reconstructed neurons in SWC format
Measuring the extent and complexity of astrocyte processes

name: CLAHE
description: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization for enhancing local contrast in images. Particularly useful for images with varying illumination or low contrast regions.
input_data: 8-bit grayscale 2D images with poor or uneven contrast
output_data: Contrast-enhanced image with improved local visibility of features
use_when: You need to enhance local contrast in images with varying illumination, reveal details in both bright and dark regions simultaneously, or improve visibility for visual inspection
do_not_use_when: You need to preserve original intensity values for quantitative measurements, global contrast adjustment is sufficient (use Brightness/Contrast), or image already has good contrast
typical_use_cases: Enhancing contrast in H&E-stained histology images for visual inspection
Improving visibility of structures in low-contrast microscopy images
Preprocessing images with uneven illumination before manual analysis
Enhancing details in both bright and dark regions of electron microscopy images

name: Classic Watershed
description: Traditional watershed segmentation algorithm for separating touching objects. Works on grayscale images using intensity gradients to find object boundaries.
input_data: Grayscale 2D images (typically gradient or distance transform images) where intensity represents topography
output_data: Label image with each catchment basin assigned a unique label, separated by watershed lines
use_when: You need watershed segmentation on grayscale images (not binary), want to use intensity gradients as the landscape for watershed, or need more control than binary watershed
do_not_use_when: You have binary images (use Distance Transform Watershed), objects are very irregular (use Watershed Irregular Features), or you need marker-controlled watershed to prevent over-segmentation
typical_use_cases: Segmenting cells using gradient magnitude as watershed input
Separating touching objects in distance-transformed binary images
Watershed segmentation of stained tissue sections
Splitting merged objects using intensity valleys as boundaries

name: Distance Transform Watershed
description: Watershed segmentation based on distance transform of binary images. Excellent for separating round touching objects like cells or nuclei.
input_data: Binary 2D images with touching round or convex objects
output_data: Label image where each separated object has a unique integer ID
use_when: You need to separate touching round objects (cells, nuclei) in binary images, as distance transform peaks correspond to object centers
do_not_use_when: Objects are highly elongated or irregular (use Watershed Irregular Features), objects are not touching, or you need grayscale watershed
typical_use_cases: Separating touching nuclei after thresholding DAPI-stained images
Splitting clustered round cells in binary masks
Post-processing binary segmentation to individualize round objects
Preparing binary masks for per-object measurements

name: FilamentDetector
description: Detects and traces filamentous structures like actin, microtubules, or other cytoskeletal elements. Provides length, curvature, and intensity measurements.
input_data: 2D fluorescence images containing filamentous/linear structures (actin, microtubules, intermediate filaments)
output_data: Detected filament paths with measurements (length, curvature, mean intensity), filament overlay image
use_when: You need to detect, trace, and measure individual filaments or linear structures in 2D fluorescence images
do_not_use_when: Structures are branching trees (use SNT for neurons), you only need orientation analysis (use OrientationJ), or structures are in 3D
typical_use_cases: Measuring actin filament lengths in fluorescence images
Quantifying microtubule curvature and straightness
Detecting and counting individual filaments in cytoskeleton images
Measuring intensity along traced filaments

name: FLIMJ
description: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging analysis plugin. Fits fluorescence decay curves to extract lifetime parameters for FLIM-FRET and metabolic imaging applications.
input_data: FLIM datasets (fluorescence decay curves per pixel), typically from time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) or frequency-domain FLIM systems
output_data: Lifetime maps, fitted decay parameters (tau, amplitude, chi-squared), FRET efficiency maps
use_when: You have FLIM data and need to fit fluorescence decay curves, extract lifetime values, compute FRET efficiencies, or create lifetime maps
do_not_use_when: You have standard intensity-based fluorescence images (not FLIM), data is not time-resolved, or you need simple intensity colocalization
typical_use_cases: Fitting fluorescence lifetime decay curves from TCSPC-FLIM data
Creating lifetime maps to visualize protein-protein interactions via FRET
Analyzing metabolic state via NADH/FAD lifetime changes
Quantifying FRET efficiency from donor lifetime changes

name: Kappa
description: Curvature analysis tool for measuring bending and curvature of filamentous structures. Provides local curvature values along traced curves.
input_data: 2D images containing curved filamentous structures that can be traced as curves (B-splines)
output_data: Curvature values along traced curves, curvature plots, point-by-point curvature measurements
use_when: You need to measure local curvature or bending of filamentous structures along their length, such as cytoskeletal elements or membrane contours
do_not_use_when: You only need filament length (use FilamentDetector), structures are straight, or you need global orientation rather than local curvature (use OrientationJ)
typical_use_cases: Measuring curvature of microtubules under different conditions
Analyzing membrane bending at sites of endocytosis
Quantifying filament stiffness through curvature measurements
Measuring local curvature along cell boundaries or contours

name: MaMuT
description: Massive Multi-view Tracker for large-scale cell tracking in multi-view light-sheet microscopy data. Handles terabyte-scale datasets with manual and semi-automatic tracking.
input_data: Large multi-view light-sheet microscopy time-lapse datasets (BigDataViewer format, HDF5/XML)
output_data: Cell tracks, lineage trees, division events, track statistics (velocity, displacement)
use_when: You need to track cells in large multi-view light-sheet data, build cell lineage trees, or track divisions in developing embryos
do_not_use_when: Data is 2D (use TrackMate or Manual Tracking), data is small enough for standard tracking tools, or you don't need lineage/division tracking
typical_use_cases: Tracking cells during embryonic development in light-sheet data
Building lineage trees with cell division tracking
Semi-automatic cell tracking in multi-view time-lapse data
Analyzing cell migration patterns in large 3D+time datasets

name: MoBIE
description: Multi-modal Big Image Explorer for browsing and annotating large multi-modal imaging datasets. Integrates with BigDataViewer for seamless navigation.
input_data: Large multi-modal datasets (EM, light microscopy, segmentations, annotations) stored in N5/Zarr/HDF5 format, typically organized as a MoBIE project
output_data: Interactive multi-modal visualization, annotations, bookmarks, table-linked views
use_when: You need to browse, explore, and annotate large multi-modal imaging datasets (e.g. correlative EM/LM), or create interactive atlases
do_not_use_when: Data is a single small image, you don't need multi-modal overlay, or you need quantitative analysis rather than exploration
typical_use_cases: Browsing correlative electron and light microscopy datasets
Exploring tissue atlases with overlaid segmentations and annotations
Navigating large connectomics datasets with linked tables
Creating shareable multi-modal image projects for collaboration

name: Multi-Template Matching
description: Finds multiple instances of a template pattern in images. Uses normalized cross-correlation with non-maximum suppression for detecting repeated structures.
input_data: 2D image to search in, plus one or more template images (cropped examples of the object to find)
output_data: Detected positions (XY coordinates) and correlation scores for each match, ROI set marking detections
use_when: You need to find multiple instances of a known pattern or object in an image, especially when objects have consistent appearance but varying positions
do_not_use_when: Objects vary significantly in appearance, size, or rotation (use feature-based detection), you need semantic segmentation, or templates are not available
typical_use_cases: Detecting repeated structures (wells, colonies, landmarks) in plate images
Finding multiple cells with similar morphology using a template crop
Locating fiducial markers or registration landmarks across images
Counting objects with consistent appearance by template matching

name: NeuronJ
description: Semi-automatic neuron tracing tool for 2D images. Uses live-wire algorithm for efficient tracing of neuronal processes with length and branching measurements.
input_data: 2D grayscale or fluorescence images of neurons with visible processes (dendrites, axons)
output_data: Traced neurite paths with length measurements, branching topology, exportable tracings
use_when: You need semi-automatic neuron tracing in 2D images with the live-wire algorithm for guided tracing along neurite paths
do_not_use_when: Data is 3D (use SNT), you need fully automatic tracing, or structures are not neuron-like (use FilamentDetector for cytoskeleton)
typical_use_cases: Tracing neurites in 2D fluorescence images of cultured neurons
Measuring total neurite length per cell in differentiation assays
Quantifying branching complexity in 2D neuronal images
Semi-automated tracing with live-wire guidance along neurite paths

name: Time Stamper
description: Adds timestamps or frame numbers to time-lapse movies. Customizable position, format, font, and time units for annotating temporal sequences.
input_data: Time-lapse image stacks or movies
output_data: Annotated image stack with timestamps or frame numbers burned into each frame
use_when: You need to add time annotations, frame numbers, or elapsed time labels to time-lapse movies for presentations or publications
do_not_use_when: You need to add spatial scale bars (use Scale Bar tool), timestamps are already embedded, or you need non-destructive overlays
typical_use_cases: Adding elapsed time labels to live-cell imaging movies
Stamping frame numbers on time-lapse sequences for reference
Annotating movies with custom time formats (hh:mm:ss, seconds) for presentations
Adding timestamps before exporting movies as AVI or MP4

name: TransformJ
description: Collection of geometric transformation tools including translation, rotation, scaling, shearing, and affine transforms for 2D and 3D images.
input_data: 2D or 3D images that need geometric transformations
output_data: Transformed image (rotated, scaled, translated, sheared, or affine-transformed)
use_when: You need precise geometric transformations (arbitrary rotation angles, scaling, affine) with control over interpolation method
do_not_use_when: You need image registration (use StackReg or bUnwarpJ), built-in Image > Transform operations are sufficient, or you need elastic deformations
typical_use_cases: Rotating images by arbitrary angles with high-quality interpolation
Applying known affine transformation matrices to images
Scaling images with specific interpolation methods (linear, cubic, quintic)
Geometric correction of tilted or skewed microscopy images

name: DiameterJ
description: Analyzes fiber diameter distributions in SEM and other microscopy images. Segments fibers and measures diameter histograms for nanofiber characterization.
input_data: SEM or microscopy images of fibrous materials (electrospun nanofibers, collagen, etc.), pre-segmented or raw
output_data: Diameter distribution histograms, mean/median diameter, fiber orientation distribution, segmented fiber image
use_when: You need to measure fiber diameter distributions in images of fibrous materials, especially electrospun nanofibers or similar networks
do_not_use_when: Fibers are isolated (can measure manually), structures are not fibrous, or you need single-fiber tracing (use FilamentDetector)
typical_use_cases: Measuring nanofiber diameter distributions from SEM images
Characterizing electrospun scaffold fiber sizes
Analyzing collagen fiber diameters in tissue sections
Quality control of fiber-based biomaterials

name: SIMcheck
description: Quality control plugin for Structured Illumination Microscopy data. Assesses raw data quality and reconstruction artifacts in SIM images.
input_data: Raw SIM data (multiple orientations and phases) and/or reconstructed SIM images
output_data: Quality metrics (modulation contrast, Fourier plots, motion/illumination checks), artifact assessment reports
use_when: You have SIM data and need to assess raw data quality before reconstruction, check for reconstruction artifacts, or validate SIM image reliability
do_not_use_when: Data is not from SIM microscopy, you need to process standard widefield or confocal images, or you need quality control for other super-resolution methods (STORM, PALM)
typical_use_cases: Checking raw SIM data quality (modulation contrast, pattern visibility)
Detecting reconstruction artifacts in SIM images (honeycomb patterns, hallucinations)
Assessing motion artifacts in SIM time-lapse data
Validating SIM reconstruction parameters before publication

name: TAPAS
description: Towards an Automated Processing and Analysis System. Framework for building automated image analysis pipelines with batch processing capabilities.
input_data: Any images supported by ImageJ/Fiji, with pipeline configuration files defining processing steps
output_data: Processed images and measurement results as defined by the pipeline configuration
use_when: You need to build reproducible, automated image analysis pipelines with batch processing, or want a framework for chaining processing steps without writing macros
do_not_use_when: You only need to process a single image interactively, the pipeline is simple enough for a macro, or you need GPU acceleration (use CLIJ2)
typical_use_cases: Building automated batch processing pipelines for image analysis
Creating reproducible workflows with configuration files
Chaining multiple analysis steps (segmentation, measurement, filtering) into a pipeline
Processing large batches of images with consistent parameters

name: Marker-controlled Watershed
description: Watershed segmentation using user-defined or automatic markers to control the segmentation. Prevents over-segmentation by seeding from marker positions.
input_data: Grayscale or gradient image plus a marker/seed image (label image with one label per object to segment)
output_data: Label image where each region is assigned to its nearest marker, separated by watershed lines
use_when: Standard watershed over-segments your image and you have (or can generate) marker positions for each object to prevent over-segmentation
do_not_use_when: You don't have markers and can't generate them, objects are simple enough for thresholding, or you need interactive marker placement (use Interactive Marker-controlled Watershed)
typical_use_cases: Segmenting cells using detected nuclei as markers to define cell boundaries
Preventing over-segmentation by seeding watershed from known object centers
Using local maxima of distance transform as markers for separating touching objects
Combining nucleus detection (StarDist) with marker-controlled watershed for cell segmentation

name: Interactive Marker-controlled Watershed
description: Interactive version of marker-controlled watershed where users can add/remove markers and see segmentation results in real-time.
input_data: 2D grayscale or gradient image where you want to interactively place markers to guide watershed segmentation
output_data: Label image with watershed-segmented regions, controlled by user-placed markers
use_when: You need interactive control over watershed segmentation, want to manually place/adjust markers and see results in real-time, or are optimizing marker placement
do_not_use_when: You need automated batch processing (use Marker-controlled Watershed), markers can be generated programmatically, or images don't need watershed segmentation
typical_use_cases: Interactively segmenting tissue regions by placing markers
Manually correcting over- or under-segmentation by adding/removing markers
Exploring optimal marker placement for watershed segmentation
Segmenting a small number of images where manual guidance is feasible

name: RATS
description: Robust Automatic Threshold Selection using gradient information. Automatically determines thresholds for images with varying background or noise levels.
input_data: 2D grayscale images, especially those with varying background, noise, or low contrast between foreground and background
output_data: Binary image with automatically determined threshold, adapted to local image properties
use_when: Standard global and local thresholding methods fail, the image has spatially varying noise or contrast, or you need a robust threshold that considers gradient information
do_not_use_when: Standard Auto Threshold or Auto Local Threshold methods work well, image has uniform contrast, or you need deep-learning-based segmentation
typical_use_cases: Thresholding images where standard methods produce inconsistent results
Segmenting structures with low contrast against a noisy background
Robust binarization of images with spatially varying noise levels
Automatic thresholding when gradient information helps distinguish foreground from background

name: CiliaQ
description: Automated analysis of primary cilia in fluorescence microscopy images. Detects, segments, and measures ciliary structures including length and intensity.
input_data: 2D/3D fluorescence images with labeled primary cilia (e.g. acetylated tubulin, Arl13b staining)
output_data: Detected cilia with measurements (length, intensity, orientation, bending), per-cilium results table
use_when: You need to detect, segment, and measure primary cilia in fluorescence microscopy images, including length, intensity, and orientation
do_not_use_when: Structures are not cilia (use FilamentDetector for other filaments), images are of motile cilia, or cilia are not fluorescently labeled
typical_use_cases: Measuring primary cilia length in cultured cells
Quantifying cilia frequency (percentage of ciliated cells)
Analyzing cilia intensity for protein localization studies
Comparing cilia morphology between control and treated conditions

name: SpermQ
description: Analysis tool for sperm flagellar beating and motility. Tracks sperm heads and analyzes flagellar waveforms for fertility research.
input_data: Time-lapse 2D dark-field or phase-contrast microscopy images of swimming sperm
output_data: Flagellar waveform parameters (beat frequency, amplitude, wavelength), head trajectory, curvature along flagellum over time
use_when: You need to analyze sperm flagellar beating patterns, measure beat frequency, or quantify sperm motility parameters
do_not_use_when: You need general cell tracking (use TrackMate), structures are not sperm, or images are not time-lapse
typical_use_cases: Measuring sperm flagellar beat frequency and amplitude
Analyzing sperm swimming patterns and head trajectories
Quantifying flagellar waveform changes under different conditions
Assessing sperm motility for fertility research

name: CARE
description: Content-Aware Image Restoration using deep learning. Restores images from noisy or underexposed acquisitions, trained on pairs of low/high quality images.
input_data: Noisy/degraded 2D/3D microscopy images, with paired low/high quality training data for model training
output_data: Restored/denoised images with improved signal-to-noise ratio while preserving structures
use_when: You have paired low/high quality training data and want to train a content-aware restoration model, or want to apply a pre-trained CARE model to noisy images
do_not_use_when: No paired training data is available (use Noise2Void/N2V instead for self-supervised denoising), images already have good quality, or you need real-time processing
typical_use_cases: Training a denoising model on paired noisy/clean image acquisitions
Restoring low-SNR live-cell imaging data using a trained CARE model
Improving image quality from fast/low-dose acquisitions
Enhancing images for downstream segmentation or analysis

name: DenoiSeg
description: Joint denoising and segmentation using deep learning. Combines image denoising with instance segmentation in a single network trained with minimal annotations.
input_data: Noisy 2D fluorescence microscopy images with a small number of segmentation annotations (only a few annotated images needed)
output_data: Denoised images and instance segmentation masks simultaneously
use_when: You have noisy images that need both denoising and segmentation, and you only have a small number of annotated training examples
do_not_use_when: Images are clean and only need segmentation (use StarDist), you have many annotations (use standard segmentation), or you only need denoising (use N2V/CARE)
typical_use_cases: Simultaneously denoising and segmenting cells with minimal annotations
Segmenting noisy live-cell images with only a few training examples
Joint restoration and segmentation when annotation budget is limited
Processing low-SNR images where noise interferes with segmentation

name: ilastik
description: Machine learning-based image segmentation, classification, and analysis. Provides interactive pixel classification, object classification, counting, and tracking through a user-friendly graphical interface backed by random forest and other ML models.
input_data: 2D or 3D grayscale or multi-channel fluorescence images where a few manual annotations (painting) can teach the classifier
output_data: Probability maps, segmentation label images, object counts, classification results, exported HDF5 datasets
use_when: You want interactive machine learning segmentation by painting labels, need pixel or object classification, or want to combine multiple image features automatically
do_not_use_when: Images have very consistent, threshold-able intensity (use Auto Threshold); you need deep learning (use StarDist or DeepImageJ); you need a purely scriptable batch pipeline
typical_use_cases: Segmenting cells in complex fluorescence images by painting foreground/background examples
Classifying tissue regions in histology images
Counting objects with overlapping intensities that defeat simple thresholding
Tracking dividing cells across time-lapse datasets

name: DeepImageJ
description: Plugin for running pre-trained deep learning models (TensorFlow, PyTorch via Torchscript) inside Fiji. Compatible with models from the BioImage Model Zoo. Enables inference without coding.
input_data: 2D or 3D images compatible with the selected pre-trained model (varies by model: fluorescence, brightfield, EM, etc.)
output_data: Model-specific outputs: segmentation masks, probability maps, denoised images, feature maps
use_when: You want to apply a published deep learning model to your images without writing code, or want to deploy your own model for others to use via the BioImage Model Zoo
do_not_use_when: No suitable pre-trained model exists for your image type; you need to train a model (use ilastik or external tools); images don't match the model's expected format
typical_use_cases: Running a published nuclei segmentation model from BioImage Model Zoo
Applying a domain-specific denoising model to fluorescence images
Deploying a custom-trained TensorFlow model for batch inference
Testing multiple pre-trained models on the same dataset

name: SAMJ
description: Segment Anything Model (SAM) integration for Fiji. Enables interactive point-and-click or bounding-box-guided segmentation of arbitrary objects in microscopy images using Meta's SAM foundation model.
input_data: Any 2D microscopy or biological image; user provides point prompts or bounding boxes to guide segmentation
output_data: ROI masks or label images for prompted objects
use_when: You need fast interactive segmentation of arbitrary objects without training a model, want zero-shot segmentation of novel object types, or need to segment a few objects quickly
do_not_use_when: You need fully automated batch segmentation of hundreds of images; objects are 3D volumes; you need sub-pixel precision measurements
typical_use_cases: Quickly segmenting a few cells or organelles by clicking on them
Annotating ground truth data for training other models
Segmenting irregular objects that defeat threshold-based methods
Rapid exploration of segmentation quality on new image types

name: Mastodon
description: Large-scale cell tracking and lineage reconstruction framework for 3D+time datasets. Built on BigDataViewer for efficient navigation of large timelapse volumes with manual curation capabilities.
input_data: 3D+time fluorescence microscopy datasets (e.g., light-sheet, confocal timelapse), typically in BDV/HDF5 format
output_data: Cell tracks, lineage trees, spot and link tables with intensity/morphology measurements
use_when: You need to track cells in 3D+time datasets too large for memory, reconstruct cell lineages, or need manual curation of automated tracks in big datasets
do_not_use_when: Data is 2D (use TrackMate), datasets fit in memory, or you need quick analysis without lineage reconstruction
typical_use_cases: Tracking nuclei in light-sheet embryo development datasets
Reconstructing cell lineage trees during zebrafish or Drosophila development
Manual proofreading of automated tracks in large 3D+time volumes
Quantifying cell division rates and fate decisions

name: JIPipe
description: Graphical batch-processing pipeline editor for Fiji. Build complex multi-step image analysis workflows as visual node graphs without writing code, supporting loops, branching, and integration of most Fiji plugins.
input_data: Any image data processable by Fiji; pipelines can handle folders of images, hyperstacks, tables
output_data: Processed images, measurements tables, reports, or any output of the integrated Fiji plugins
use_when: You need to build reproducible multi-step image analysis pipelines without coding, want a visual workflow editor, or need to run complex batch processing with branching logic
do_not_use_when: You only need a single simple operation (use built-in tools); you prefer scripting (use macros/Jython); you need real-time processing
typical_use_cases: Building a complete cell analysis pipeline: load → preprocess → segment → measure → export
Creating reproducible workflows for publication-quality analysis
Batch processing entire experiments with parameter sweeps
Teaching image analysis without requiring programming knowledge

name: GDSC SMLM2
description: Single molecule localization microscopy (SMLM) analysis suite. Provides tools for PALM/STORM/dSTORM data: molecule fitting, drift correction, clustering, diffusion analysis, and super-resolution image reconstruction.
input_data: Widefield fluorescence time-lapse images of blinking single molecules (PALM, STORM, dSTORM, DNA-PAINT datasets)
output_data: Localization tables (x,y,z coordinates, precision, intensity), super-resolution reconstructed images, diffusion coefficients, cluster statistics
use_when: You have SMLM/PALM/STORM data and need molecule fitting, drift correction, or quantitative analysis of localizations
do_not_use_when: Data is not from single-molecule localization experiments; you want standard confocal analysis; you need STED or SIM reconstruction
typical_use_cases: Fitting PSFs to blinking single molecules in dSTORM data
Correcting stage drift in long PALM acquisitions
Cluster analysis of receptor distributions on cell membranes
Measuring diffusion coefficients of membrane proteins

name: NanoJ-SRRF
description: Super-Resolution Radial Fluctuations (SRRF) algorithm for live-cell super-resolution imaging. Generates super-resolution images from short bursts of diffraction-limited frames with minimal phototoxicity.
input_data: Time-lapse burst of widefield or TIRF fluorescence images (typically 100-500 frames of the same field)
output_data: Super-resolution reconstructed image with improved spatial resolution, SQUIRREL quality map
use_when: You need live-cell super-resolution from standard fluorescence microscopes, have time-lapse bursts of diffraction-limited images, and want to avoid SMLM sample preparation requirements
do_not_use_when: You have fixed samples suitable for STORM/PALM; you need single-molecule localization data; images have very low signal-to-noise
typical_use_cases: Super-resolution imaging of live cells on a standard widefield microscope
Resolving subcellular structures below the diffraction limit in live samples
Rapid super-resolution of dynamic processes without specialized hardware
Comparing live-cell super-resolution across different acquisition conditions

name: NanoJ-Core
description: Core library and drift correction tools from the NanoJ suite for super-resolution microscopy. Provides NanoJ-Livem and cross-correlation-based drift correction for timelapse and SMLM datasets.
input_data: Timelapse fluorescence image stacks affected by stage drift
output_data: Drift-corrected image stack, drift trajectory plot
use_when: You need to correct stage drift in timelapse or SMLM acquisitions before analysis or reconstruction
do_not_use_when: Drift is negligible or already corrected; you need elastic/non-rigid registration
typical_use_cases: Correcting thermal drift in long STORM acquisitions
Pre-processing timelapse data before SRRF reconstruction
Estimating and plotting stage drift in live-cell experiments
Drift correction using fiducial markers

name: NanoJ-SQUIRREL
description: Super-resolution quality control tool. Quantifies the quality of super-resolution images by comparing them to widefield references, producing error maps and resolution-scaled Pearson coefficient metrics.
input_data: Pairs of super-resolution images and corresponding widefield/confocal reference images
output_data: Error maps, resolution-scaled Pearson coefficient (RSP), FRC resolution estimate
use_when: You need to validate the quality of super-resolution reconstructions, compare different SR methods, or detect artifacts in SR images
do_not_use_when: You don't have a reference widefield image; images are not from super-resolution experiments
typical_use_cases: Assessing quality of STORM/PALM reconstructions against widefield reference
Comparing SRRF quality under different acquisition conditions
Detecting reconstruction artifacts in super-resolution images
Reporting quantitative SR image quality for publications

name: Mastodon-DeepLineage
description: Deep learning-based lineage reconstruction extension for Mastodon. Uses neural networks to predict cell divisions and assign lineage relationships in large 3D+time datasets.
input_data: 3D+time datasets already tracked with Mastodon, with spots and links defined
output_data: Lineage tree assignments with deep-learning-predicted division events
use_when: You have Mastodon-tracked data and need automated lineage reconstruction or division detection using deep learning
do_not_use_when: Data is not in Mastodon format; you need 2D tracking; manual curation is preferred over automated lineage
typical_use_cases: Automated cell division detection in embryo development datasets
Reconstructing complete lineage trees from light-sheet data
Reducing manual curation effort in large tracking datasets

name: TrackMate-StarDist
description: TrackMate detector plugin that uses StarDist deep learning for detecting and segmenting nuclei/cells as tracking objects. Combines StarDist's accurate instance segmentation with TrackMate's tracking framework.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images with round/convex objects (nuclei, cells)
output_data: Tracked spots with StarDist segmentation outlines, track tables and statistics
use_when: You need to track nuclei in timelapse data and want StarDist's accurate detection combined with TrackMate's tracking algorithms
do_not_use_when: Objects are not round/convex; you are working with 3D volumes (use Mastodon instead); no time dimension in data
typical_use_cases: Tracking DAPI-stained nuclei through cell division in timelapse
Measuring nuclear migration speed with accurate boundary detection
Cell counting with tracking in growing colonies

name: TrackMate-Cellpose
description: TrackMate detector that uses Cellpose deep learning model for cell body segmentation and tracking. Enables tracking of whole cells (not just nuclei) using Cellpose's generalist cell segmentation.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images of cells (phase contrast, fluorescent membrane/cytoplasm markers)
output_data: Tracked cell objects with Cellpose segmentation outlines, track statistics
use_when: You need to track whole cells (not just nuclei) in timelapse using Cellpose's generalist segmentation model
do_not_use_when: You only need nucleus tracking (use TrackMate-StarDist); data is 3D; Cellpose is not installed
typical_use_cases: Tracking whole cell bodies in phase contrast timelapse
Measuring cell migration with accurate cell boundary delineation
Tracking irregularly shaped cells that StarDist handles poorly

name: TrackMate-Weka
description: TrackMate detector using Trainable Weka Segmentation for object detection. Allows training a custom pixel classifier to detect objects and use it as a TrackMate detector.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images; requires a pre-trained Weka classifier
output_data: Tracked objects detected via Weka classifier, track tables
use_when: You need a custom-trained detector for non-standard object types in TrackMate, and already have or can train a Weka classifier
do_not_use_when: Standard detectors (StarDist, LoG) work adequately; you don't have training data for Weka
typical_use_cases: Detecting non-round objects that standard detectors miss
Tracking objects with complex texture features
Reusing existing Weka classifiers in a tracking pipeline

name: TrackMate-Ilastik
description: TrackMate detector using ilastik pixel classification for object detection. Links TrackMate tracking framework with ilastik's interactive machine learning classifier.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images; requires a pre-trained ilastik pixel classification project
output_data: Tracked objects from ilastik-classified regions, track statistics
use_when: You have an existing ilastik classifier and want to use it for detection within TrackMate's tracking framework
do_not_use_when: You don't have ilastik installed or a trained classifier; standard TrackMate detectors work for your data
typical_use_cases: Detecting cells with complex appearance using ilastik probability maps
Integrating ilastik-based segmentation into a tracking pipeline

name: TrackMate-MorphoLibJ
description: TrackMate integration with MorphoLibJ for label-image-based tracking. Uses MorphoLibJ-generated label images as input for defining tracked objects in TrackMate.
input_data: Pre-segmented label images from MorphoLibJ alongside the original timelapse images
output_data: Tracked objects derived from label regions, track tables and spot measurements
use_when: You have segmented label images and want to track the labeled objects using TrackMate
do_not_use_when: Objects are not pre-segmented; you prefer on-the-fly detection; 3D datasets (use Mastodon)
typical_use_cases: Tracking cells already segmented by watershed in MorphoLibJ
Combining morphological segmentation with particle tracking

name: TrackMate-ExTrack
description: TrackMate action for single-particle tracking analysis using the ExTrack algorithm. Classifies trajectory segments into diffusion states (confined, free diffusion) and extracts transition rates.
input_data: Single-particle tracks from TrackMate (e.g., from live PALM or single-molecule tracking experiments)
output_data: Diffusion state classifications per trajectory segment, transition rates, diffusion coefficients per state
use_when: You have single-particle tracking data and want to classify trajectories into diffusion states and measure transition kinetics
do_not_use_when: Particles show uniform motion; you need classical MSD-only analysis; tracks are too short for state classification
typical_use_cases: Classifying receptor trajectories into free/confined diffusion states
Measuring chromatin binding/unbinding kinetics
Analyzing state transitions of membrane proteins

name: TrackMate-YOLO
description: TrackMate detector using YOLO deep learning object detection. Enables detecting and tracking objects in timelapse using YOLO's fast bounding-box detection.
input_data: 2D fluorescence or brightfield timelapse images; requires a compatible YOLO model
output_data: Tracked objects with bounding-box detections, track tables
use_when: You have a YOLO model trained on your object type and want fast detection-based tracking in TrackMate
do_not_use_when: No YOLO model available for your objects; you need precise pixel-level segmentation; standard detectors work
typical_use_cases: Fast tracking using a pre-trained YOLO cell detector
Detecting and tracking multiple object classes simultaneously

name: TrackMate-Spotiflow
description: TrackMate detector using Spotiflow deep learning for spot/puncta detection. Optimized for detecting sub-resolution spots (mRNA, vesicles, foci) with high precision in fluorescence images.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images with small spot-like objects (mRNA spots, vesicles, protein foci)
output_data: Tracked spots detected by Spotiflow, localization tables, track statistics
use_when: You need to detect and track sub-resolution fluorescent spots (mRNA, vesicles, foci) with high accuracy
do_not_use_when: Objects are large cells or nuclei; you need simple intensity-based spot detection (use LoG detector)
typical_use_cases: Tracking mRNA spots in live smFISH experiments
Following vesicle trajectories in TIRF microscopy
Quantifying foci dynamics in response to DNA damage

name: TrackMate-Lacss
description: TrackMate detector using LACSS deep learning model for cell segmentation-based tracking. LACSS is a point-supervised cell segmentation model.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images of cells
output_data: Tracked cells with LACSS segmentation outlines, track tables
use_when: You want to use the LACSS model for cell detection and tracking within TrackMate
do_not_use_when: LACSS is not suitable for your cell type; other TrackMate detectors work better
typical_use_cases: Cell tracking using point-supervised segmentation
Tracking cells with minimal annotation requirements

name: TrackMate-Trackastra
description: TrackMate tracker using Trackastra transformer-based tracking. Uses attention mechanisms for robust multi-object tracking across frames.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images with detected spots/objects from any TrackMate detector
output_data: Object tracks linked by the Trackastra neural network tracker
use_when: Standard TrackMate linkers fail in crowded scenes; you want deep learning-based track linking
do_not_use_when: Simple nearest-neighbor linking works for your data; you have very sparse objects
typical_use_cases: Tracking in dense cell populations where LAP linker fails
Handling complex cell trajectories with crossings

name: TrackMate-Helper
description: Utility extension for TrackMate that adds helper functions for batch processing, parameter optimization, and automated analysis of TrackMate results.
input_data: Multiple timelapse image datasets for batch TrackMate analysis
output_data: Batch tracking results, parameter optimization reports
use_when: You need to run TrackMate on multiple datasets automatically or optimize tracking parameters systematically
do_not_use_when: You only have a single dataset to analyze manually
typical_use_cases: Batch tracking of multiple experimental conditions
Automated parameter optimization for TrackMate

name: TrackMate-GEFF
description: TrackMate extension for exporting tracking data to GEFF (Generic Edge/Feature Format), enabling interoperability with other tracking analysis tools.
input_data: TrackMate tracking results (spots, edges, tracks)
output_data: GEFF format files for downstream analysis in other tools
use_when: You need to export TrackMate results to GEFF format for analysis in other software
do_not_use_when: You only need standard CSV/XML export from TrackMate
typical_use_cases: Exporting tracks for analysis in Python or R tracking libraries
Cross-tool comparison of tracking results

name: TrackMate-InTRACKtive
description: TrackMate visualization extension providing interactive web-based visualization of tracking results using the InTRACKtive viewer.
input_data: TrackMate tracking results
output_data: Interactive web-based visualization of tracks
use_when: You want an interactive web browser-based visualization of your TrackMate tracks
do_not_use_when: Simple table/plot outputs are sufficient; no web browser available
typical_use_cases: Sharing tracking results with collaborators via web viewer
Interactive exploration of complex track datasets

name: TrackMate-Kymograph
description: TrackMate action for generating kymographs from tracked objects. Creates space-time plots along track trajectories for visualizing dynamics.
input_data: TrackMate tracks from timelapse images
output_data: Kymograph images showing intensity along track trajectories over time
use_when: You want to visualize the intensity or signal along tracked object trajectories as kymographs
do_not_use_when: Tracks are too short or too dense for meaningful kymographs
typical_use_cases: Visualizing cargo movement along axons
Showing vesicle dynamics along microtubules

name: TrackMate-Oneat
description: TrackMate detector using Oneat deep learning for mitosis detection and cell division event identification in timelapse images.
input_data: 2D fluorescence timelapse images showing dividing cells
output_data: Detected mitosis events, division locations and times, track splits
use_when: You need to automatically detect and annotate cell division events in timelapse data
do_not_use_when: Cells are not dividing; you only need simple spot tracking without division detection
typical_use_cases: Detecting mitosis events in phase contrast timelapse
Quantifying division rates in growing cell populations
Building lineage trees from division event detection

name: TrackMate-Pairing
description: TrackMate action for pairing tracks from different channels or conditions. Enables co-tracking and colocalization analysis of simultaneously tracked objects.
input_data: TrackMate results from two channels or conditions
output_data: Paired track tables showing associations between tracks across channels
use_when: You want to identify which objects in one channel co-move with objects in another channel
do_not_use_when: Single-channel tracking only; no paired tracking needed
typical_use_cases: Tracking cargo-motor co-movement in dual-channel TIRF
Correlating receptor and ligand trajectories

name: TrackMateCSVImporter
description: Plugin to import tracking data from CSV files into TrackMate. Enables loading external tracking results (e.g., from Python/MATLAB) into the TrackMate visualization and analysis framework.
input_data: CSV files containing spot/track data from external tracking software
output_data: TrackMate-compatible tracking data for visualization and analysis
use_when: You have tracking results from another tool and want to visualize or analyze them in TrackMate
do_not_use_when: Data is already in TrackMate XML format; you need to track from scratch
typical_use_cases: Importing Python trackpy results into TrackMate for visualization
Loading MATLAB tracking output for TrackMate analysis

name: ELEPHANT
description: Semi-automated cell tracking platform using deep learning (U-Net-based) for detecting and tracking cells in 3D+time datasets. Supports active learning with user feedback to improve detection.
input_data: 3D+time fluorescence microscopy datasets (light-sheet, confocal timelapse)
output_data: Cell detections, tracks, and lineage information with confidence scores
use_when: You need deep learning-assisted tracking of cells in 3D+time data with active learning refinement
do_not_use_when: Data is 2D (use TrackMate); fully manual tracking is preferred; no GPU available for inference
typical_use_cases: Semi-automated tracking in light-sheet embryo datasets
Active learning-guided improvement of cell detection models
3D cell tracking with user-in-the-loop refinement

name: ElastixWrapper
description: Fiji wrapper for the Elastix image registration toolkit. Provides access to Elastix's full registration capabilities including rigid, affine, b-spline deformable, and diffeomorphic registration with multiple similarity metrics.
input_data: 2D or 3D fixed/moving image pairs for registration
output_data: Registered images, transformation parameters, deformation fields
use_when: You need powerful multi-modal image registration, complex deformable registration, or want to use Elastix's full parameter control from within Fiji
do_not_use_when: Simple rigid registration is sufficient (use StackReg); you need elastic consistent registration (use bUnwarpJ)
typical_use_cases: Registering pre/post-treatment 3D fluorescence volumes
Multi-modal registration (fluorescence to EM)
Deformable registration of histology serial sections
Atlas-based registration of tissue samples

name: MOSAIC ToolSuite
description: Collection of image processing and analysis tools from MPI-CBG Dresden, including particle tracking, image segmentation (Region Competition), and background subtraction (PPM).
input_data: 2D/3D fluorescence images for tracking or segmentation tasks
output_data: Particle tracks, segmented regions, background-subtracted images
use_when: You need MPI-CBG's particle tracking algorithms, region competition segmentation, or their background subtraction methods
do_not_use_when: You need deep-learning-based tracking; simple nearest-neighbor linking is sufficient
typical_use_cases: Tracking fluorescent particles in live cells
Segmenting cells via region competition energy minimization
Removing heterogeneous background in fluorescence images

name: Colocalization by Cross Correlation
description: Statistically rigorous colocalization analysis plugin using cross-correlation functions. Computes colocalization as a function of spatial displacement to account for point spread function effects and provide confidence intervals.
input_data: Two-channel fluorescence images where colocalization between channels is to be quantified
output_data: Cross-correlation curves, colocalization metrics with confidence intervals, colocalization maps
use_when: You need statistically rigorous colocalization analysis that accounts for PSF broadening and provides confidence intervals, avoiding pitfalls of simple pixel-based colocalization
do_not_use_when: You need simple Pearson/Manders coefficients (use Coloc 2); images are not two-channel fluorescence
typical_use_cases: Measuring protein-protein colocalization in super-resolution images
Quantifying proximity of two fluorescent labels in confocal data
Testing statistical significance of colocalization

name: GDSC
description: Collection of image analysis plugins from the Genome Damage and Stability Centre (GDSC), University of Sussex. Includes tools for fluorescence image analysis, spot counting, cell tracking, and microscopy quality control.
input_data: Fluorescence microscopy images for spot detection, cell analysis, or quality assessment
output_data: Spot counts, cell measurements, quality metrics tables
use_when: You need GDSC-developed tools for spot counting, cell analysis, or microscope quality assessment
do_not_use_when: You specifically need SMLM analysis (use GDSC-SMLM2)
typical_use_cases: Counting DNA damage foci in fluorescence images
Assessing microscope PSF and image quality
Batch fluorescence spot counting

name: clij2
description: Extended GPU-accelerated image processing library building on CLIJ. CLIJ2 provides over 300 GPU-accelerated operations for filtering, segmentation, measurement, and mathematical operations using OpenCL.
input_data: Any 2D/3D image processable by ImageJ, pushed to GPU memory
